Client: Thanks. I’m trying to make sure I’m not missing anything—income, taxes, and whether I’m set up right now that it’s just me.
Advisor: Got it. We’ll keep it practical and step-by-step. Let’s start with the big picture: what’s your household gross income in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All in, my gross income is 166450 a year.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. And that’s coming from pensions, right? Can you walk me through the two pieces so I understand what’s steady versus more variable?
Client: Yeah. One pension is 53700 annually, pretty steady.

Client: And then there’s another pension piece that’s more irregular—if you annualize it, it’s 112700, but it doesn’t show up evenly.
Advisor: Perfect, that helps. Switching to spending for a second—about what are your total monthly expenses, just to run the cash-flow math?
Client: I’m around 5850 a month in total expenses.
Advisor: Okay. And any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards—anything like that?
Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client: Thanks. I’m trying to make sure I’m not missing anything—income, taxes, and whether I’m set up right now that it’s just me.

Advisor: Okay—thank you. And that’s coming from pensions, right? Can you walk me through the two pieces so I understand what’s steady versus more variable?
Client: Yeah. One pension is 53700 annually, pretty steady.

Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk assets next—investments and accounts you can draw from. Roughly what do your investable assets total today?
Client: Investable assets total 602100.
Advisor: Thanks. I’m going to break that into the buckets you mentioned so we don’t miss anything. First, you’ve got a taxable investment account—what’s that sitting at?
Client: The taxable account is 257150.

Client: That’s 103000.
Advisor: Got it. And then your home—just so we capture the full picture—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client: 146550.
Advisor: Thanks. On the liability side, just confirming: you don’t have any loans outstanding at all right now?
Client: Correct—no loans outstanding.

Advisor: Great. One sec—just noting that. Since you mentioned next time you want taxes and what to draw from first, is it fair to say you’re mostly thinking about how those two pension streams interact with withdrawals?
Client: Yeah, exactly. I’m in a 37% bracket and I don’t want to create a mess with extra taxable income if I don’t have to.
Advisor: Makes sense. Next meeting we’ll map a simple order of operations—what to spend first, what to leave alone, and what that does to taxes.
Advisor: For today, last check: liabilities are all zeros—no loans, no mortgage, no other debt—and your property value is in the file. Anything else debt-related you can think of, Michael?
